title: Attention Is All You Need
text: We propose the Transformer, a model architecture eschewing recurrence and instead relying entirely on an attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output. The Transformer allows for significantly more parallelization and can reach a new state of the art in translation quality after being trained for as little as twelve hours on eight P100 GPUs. We trained on the standard WMT 2014 English-German dataset consisting of about 4.5 million sentence pairs and the WMT 2014 English-French dataset consisting of 36M sentences. We used byte-pair encoding with a shared source-target vocabulary of about 37000 tokens. We evaluated using beam search with a beam size of 4 and length penalty alpha=0.6. The primary limitation of this approach is that self-attention has quadratic complexity in sequence length, which makes processing very long documents expensive. We did not evaluate on documents longer than 512 tokens due to memory constraints. Statistical significance was evaluated using bootstrap resampling with p < 0.001.

expected.methodology: Transformer architecture using multi-head self-attention without recurrence or convolution, trained with Adam optimizer and label smoothing
key_findings: Transformer achieves 28.4 BLEU on EN-DE translation
Trained in 12 hours on 8 P100 GPUs
Outperforms all previous architectures including ensembles
limitations: Memory constraints limit sequences beyond 512 tokens

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
text: We introduce BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. BERT is pre-trained on BooksCorpus (800M words) and English Wikipedia (2,500M words). We use a masked language modeling objective where 15% of tokens are randomly masked. We evaluate on GLUE, SQuAD v1.1, SQuAD v2.0, and SWAG benchmarks. BERT-Large achieves 93.2% on SQuAD 2.0 and 86.7% on GLUE, outperforming previous methods by a large margin. A limitation of our approach is that the masked language model objective creates a mismatch between pre-training and fine-tuning since the [MASK] token does not appear during fine-tuning. McNemar's test was used for statistical significance testing.

expected.methodology: Bidirectional transformer pre-trained with masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP) objectives
key_findings: Bidirectional context improves over unidirectional models
limitations: [MASK] token mismatch between pre-training and fine-tuning

title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
text: We propose Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), which freezes the pre-trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture. For a pre-trained weight matrix W in R^{d x k}, we constrain its update by representing it with a low-rank decomposition W + BA where B is in R^{d x r} and A is in R^{r x k}, with rank r << min(d, k). We apply LoRA to GPT-3 175B and evaluate on WikiSQL and MultiNLI. LoRA with r=4 matches or outperforms full fine-tuning while reducing trainable parameters by 10,000x and GPU memory requirement by 3x. Training was performed on 4 A100 80GB GPUs. Limitations include that LoRA does not reduce inference latency when adapters are not merged back into the base weights. We report mean accuracy over three random seeds; paired t-test with p < 0.05 was used for significance testing.

expected.methodology: Low-rank decomposition of weight update matrices, freezing pretrained weights and training only the low-rank adapter matrices A and B
key_findings: Matches full fine-tuning with 10,000x fewer trainable parameters
limitations: Does not reduce inference latency unless adapters are merged
Requires choosing rank r as a hyperparameter

title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
text: We introduce Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a stable, performant, and computationally lightweight algorithm for training language models from human preferences without reinforcement learning. DPO implicitly defines a reward model whose optimal policy can be extracted in closed form, allowing us to solve the standard RLHF problem with only a simple binary cross-entropy objective. We evaluate DPO on sentiment modulation, summarization (TL;DR), and single-turn dialogue (Anthropic HH). DPO uses the Anthropic HH dataset (161K comparisons) and TL;DR dataset (93K examples). DPO matches or exceeds PPO-based methods in all settings while being significantly simpler to implement. A key limitation is that DPO requires paired preference data; it cannot learn from scalar reward signals alone. We report win rates from GPT-4 evaluation across 200 held-out prompts.

expected.methodology: DPO: binary cross-entropy loss derived from the closed-form optimal policy for RLHF, eliminates the need for explicit reward model training and RL
key_findings: Matches or exceeds PPO in all evaluated settings
limitations: Cannot learn from scalar reward signals alone

title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
text: We present QLoRA, an efficient finetuning approach that reduces memory usage enough to finetune a 65B parameter model on a single 48GB GPU while preserving full 16-bit finetuning task performance. QLoRA backpropagates gradients through a frozen, 4-bit quantized pretrained language model into Low Rank Adapters (LoRA). We introduce 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4), a new data type optimal for normally distributed weights, and double quantization to reduce memory footprint. We finetune models on FLAN v2 and evaluate on Vicuna benchmark using GPT-4 scoring. Our best model, Guanaco, outperforms ChatGPT on 99.3% of prompts in human evaluation. Training used a single A100 80GB GPU for 24 hours. Limitations include that 4-bit quantization introduces quantization error and NF4 requires custom CUDA kernels. Human raters evaluated 200 prompts; inter-rater agreement measured by Cohen's kappa.

expected.methodology: 4-bit NF4 quantization of base model combined with LoRA adapters; double quantization of quantization constants; paged optimizers for memory spikes
key_findings: Guanaco outperforms ChatGPT on 99.3% of prompts
NF4 is optimal data type for normally distributed LLM weights
